title: The Second Sunday after the Epiphany
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
January 22, 2019 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Second Sunday after the Epiphany in the Christian calendar, falling within the season after Epiphany (which runs from January 6 through the Sunday before Ash Wednesday). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would likely include 1 Samuel 3:1-10, Psalm 139:1-5 or 8-10, 1 Corinthians 6:12-20, and John 1:43-51—themes centered on calling, vocation, and discipleship. This Sunday emphasizes Jesus calling his first disciples and the theme of responding to God's call in one's life.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a period of significant internal tension over LGBTQ+ inclusion and marriage equality, with the 2018 General Convention (held in Austin, Texas in July 2018) having recently affirmed more inclusive language for marriage rites. The Diocese of Colorado, led by Bishop Robert J. Wright since 2017, was navigating these evolving national church positions within a moderately progressive Rocky Mountain context. No major diocesan leadership changes or crises were occurring at this specific moment.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical winter conditions with cold temperatures in the mid-30s Fahrenheit. The city's political focus was on newly inaugurated Mayor Michael Hancock (sworn in May 2019, but campaign momentum was visible) and ongoing discussions about homelessness and housing affordability in Capitol Hill. No major local disasters or breaking news events marked the week immediately preceding January 22.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The partial federal government shutdown (which began December 22, 2018) was ongoing and dominated headlines—it would last until January 25, creating uncertainty about federal employees and services. The 2020 presidential campaign was beginning to take shape with early candidate announcements. The Women's March on Washington had occurred on January 19, 2019 (three days prior), drawing attention to gender equality and reproductive rights issues.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The humanitarian crisis in Venezuela under Nicolás Maduro was intensifying, with opposition leader Juan Guaidó gaining international recognition—this would become more acute in the following weeks but was already a significant story. The ongoing Syrian civil war continued with Russian and Iranian involvement, and Brexit negotiations remained turbulent in the United Kingdom as the March 29 deadline approached.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was one of political polarization and uncertainty tied to the shutdown, with Americans divided along partisan lines. Popular culture was focused on the lead-up to the Super Bowl (scheduled for February 3, 2019, featuring the New England Patriots and Los Angeles Rams). The #MeToo movement was still reshaping conversations about power and accountability, particularly visible in the recent Women's March messaging.
